reasoning: The baseline CDC estimate of U.S. deaths due to antibiotic-resistant infections has been anchored at 35,000 since its widely cited 2019 report. Recent CDC website updates through early to mid-2025 continue to cite this 35,000 figure. Even during the peak of the COVID-19 pandemic, the CDC reported an increase of approximately 20% in certain hospital-onset resistant infections, which would raise deaths to around 42,000—still far short of the 70,000 threshold required for resolution. A secondary global study by the GRAM project (IHME/Oxford) updated global forecasts in late 2024 but has not generated any headline-grabbing U.S.-specific metric of more than 70,000 deaths. Older alternative estimates (such as a 2018 Washington University paper claiming ~160k deaths) have evidently not triggered a YES resolution, likely due to Metaculus credulity standards or the focus on institutional reports like those from the CDC. With less than five months remaining until the December 31, 2025 resolution constraint, the chance of the CDC or an equivalent institution publishing a radically revised methodology that abruptly doubles the accepted U.S. annual mortality count is exceedingly low. The community sits at 8%, but the lack of recent catalysts and the narrowing time window support an even lower prediction.
